fact: August 19 is exactly 231 days into the year, which means there are only 134 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for cicadas to be screaming their loudest - some can buzz as loud as a motorcycle!
The sun rises about 2 minutes later than it did just a week ago, and summer is secretly sneaking away minute by minute.
August 19 falls right in the middle of Leo season, when the zodiac lion is roaring at full strength until August 22.
Corn mazes are typically being carved right now because corn stalks hit their perfect towering height in mid-August in many northern regions.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, August 19 is deep winter, and some penguins are huddling together in temperatures that could freeze your spit mid-air.
The Perseid meteor shower just finished a few days ago, but space dust is still floating around making extra-sparkly shooting stars.
Tree leaves in northern places are secretly starting to slow down their green chlorophyll production, even though they still look perfectly green.
August 19 is roughly 33 days before the autumn equinox, when day and night will be exactly the same length everywhere on Earth.
In many places, this is when grasshoppers and crickets are at their jumpiest and noisiest, creating nature's own outdoor concert every evening.
The ocean water in the Northern Hemisphere has been soaking up summer heat for months and is now typically at its warmest and most perfect for swimming.

Sunflowers in northern regions are usually reaching their peak giant-ness right now, with some growing taller than basketball players and turning their faces to follow the sun like slow-motion dancers.
